1:
The chapter opens on a national crisis, and the witnesses divide their labour between establishing who this Benhadad was and drawing the lesson of Israel's sudden helplessness. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the history. Charles Ellicott explains that Ben-hadad is not a personal name but the inherited title of the Syrian kings, 'son of Hadad,' and infers from the later allusion in v.34 that this king was the son of one who had already been victorious against Omri; Damascus, once subordinate, has now assumed 'a most formidable predominance,' so that Ahab cannot stand before it but shuts himself up, probably after a defeat, within the strong walls of Samaria. John Gill agrees that this is Benhadad the second, son of the Benhadad whom Asa had once hired against Baasha (1 Kings 15:18-20). On the thirty-two kings both concur with Adam Clarke: these were tributary chieftains, for in that age 'every town and city had its independent chieftain,' confederated under a leading power.

The puritanMatthew Henry reads the invasion as providence rather than mere politics. What the ground of the quarrel was Scripture does not say, but the deeper cause is plain: David had subdued the Syrians and made them tributaries, and only 'Israel's apostasy from God makes them formidable again.' Surely, Henry says, Israel's defence had departed from them, or the Syrians could not have marched so readily to Samaria, 'the head and heart of the country.' He notices too the folly Asa began in tempting Syria to invade at all: 'it is dangerous bringing a foreign force into the country; posterity may pay dearly for it.'

All the witnesses feel the sting of the title king so cheaply multiplied. 'How little did the title of king look,' Henry exclaims, 'when all these poor petty governors pretended to it!' The thirty-two crowned heads who will presently be drinking in Benhadad's train become, for the puritan reading, an emblem of pomp without substance — the very pride God is about to abase.

Beneath the history the expositors land on one pastoral point, pressed most fully by Henry: sin puts a people out of the divine protection. 'If God may not rule us, our enemies shall. A rebel to God is a slave to all besides.' Ahab had prepared his silver and gold for Baal (Hosea 2:8), and now it is justly demanded of him by a heathen, for such alienating of what belongs to God 'amounts to a forfeiture.' Thus the opening verse is read not merely as the record of an aggression but as the working of a settled law — that guilt disarms, and a nation which will not be defended by the Lord finds no other shield when the Syrian bands come up.
2:
The first embassy states Benhadad's terms, and the witnesses read it as the formal assertion of Syrian overlordship over Israel. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the diplomatic frame. Keil and Delitzsch render the demand plainly — 'thy silver and thy gold are mine, and the best of thy wives and thy sons are mine' — and note that Ahab answered 'with pusillanimity,' a submissiveness which only made Benhadad 'still more audacious,' so that he sent a second and harsher message. Charles Ellicott reads the whole exchange as the language of suzerain and vassal: the message and Ahab's answer, 'My lord, O king,' are together 'the assertion and acceptance of Syrian sovereignty over Israel,' by which all the possessions and the very family of the vassal are acknowledged to be the property of his superior lord.

The reformedJohn Gill fastens on Ahab's posture. The king 'there remained, and attempted not to go forth and meet him,' though Benhadad must have marched through great part of his dominions to reach Samaria; the shutting up in the capital is itself a confession of weakness. What Benhadad claims he claims, in Gill's phrase on the following verse, 'not of right, but reckoning it as good as in his hands,' Ahab being unable to resist him.

The messengers are sent 'into the city,' and the witnesses agree that this detail matters: Samaria is already invested, the siege pressing, and the demand is delivered as by a conqueror who has only to name his price. Benhadad does not treat for peace between equals; he dictates. The order of the items — silver and gold first, then wives and children — moves from treasure to household, from what a man owns to what a man loves, and prepares the reader for the sharper second demand in which the searching of the very houses of Israel will be required.

There is no dispute among the few voices here; together they establish the situation on which the drama turns. A powerful heathen king, flushed with an easy advance, lays claim to everything Ahab has and is; and Ahab, having no God to lean upon and no strength of his own, prepares to yield all rather than hazard the siege — the surrender that v.4 will record in his own abject words.
3:
This verse spells out the content of the demand, and the witnesses treat it as the measure of Benhadad's arrogance and of Ahab's helplessness. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch set the words in their sequence — silver and gold, then 'thy wives also and thy children, even the goodliest, are mine' — and carry the narrative straight on to Ahab's collapse in v.4 and to the fresh, more insulting message that follows in vv.5-6. On their reading the escalation is deliberate: each concession by Ahab is met, not with satisfaction, but with a heavier claim.

The reformedJohn Gill presses the phrase 'is mine' with a lawyer's precision. Benhadad speaks of the silver and gold as his 'not of right, but reckoning it as good as in his hands, Ahab not being able to resist him.' The claim rests on nothing but force; it is the language of a robber who calls the house his because he stands armed at the door. The demand for the wives and children 'even the goodliest' turns the screw further, for it reaches past a king's treasure to the persons dearest to him, and asks him to hand over not only his wealth but his household.

The witnesses agree, with Charles Ellicott, that in the idiom of the ancient East such a total claim upon goods and family was the recognized form of a suzerain's sovereignty over his vassal: to say 'all is mine' was to say 'thou art wholly subject.' The dreadfulness of the verse lies in how much is contained in so few words — the whole substance and affection of a life laid at the feet of an insolent stranger.

Read against the chapter's later turn, the verse also serves the witnesses as a foil. The man who here dictates 'all is mine' will shortly be hiding 'in an inner chamber' (v.30) and begging for his life; and the reader is meant to remember these swollen words when the tables are turned. For now, however, the demand stands unanswered, and the expositors leave it hanging over Samaria like the threat it is, until Ahab's craven reply gives it, for a moment, the appearance of success.
4:
Ahab's answer is the low point of his courage, and the witnesses read it as a study in what sin does to a man's spirit. The words are abject — 'My lord, O king, according to thy saying, I am thine, and all that I have' — and the reformedJohn Gill notes simply that Ahab is here 'acknowledging his sovereignty over him,' owning Benhadad as lord and yielding himself and all he has without a struggle.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke looks for the calculation behind the cringe: Ahab 'probably hoped by this humiliation to soften this barbarous king, and perhaps to get better conditions.' It is the reasoning of a frightened politician who thinks that total surrender may purchase a little mercy — a hope the next message will crush.

The puritanMatthew Henry presses the moral hardest. Ahab's is 'a very disgraceful submission,' effectual though vague; and Henry traces it to two roots. First, sin had provoked God to depart, so that Benhadad could make such a demand at all: 'Sin brings men into such straits, by putting them out of divine protection.' Second, sin had wronged Ahab's own conscience and set it against him: 'Guilt dispirits men, and makes them cowards.' Knowing that Baal could not help and having no reason to think the Lord would, Ahab 'was content to buy his life upon any terms.' Henry sharpens it into a proverb of the flesh: 'Skin for skin, and all that is dear to him, he will give for it; he will rather live a beggar than not die a prince.'

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott adds a needed qualification that keeps the surrender from being read as bottomless: 'Ahab surrenders, but not at discretion.' He accepts vassalage and the loss of his goods, but he has not yet handed Samaria over to be sacked at pleasure; there is a line, dimly felt, that even this abject king will not cross. It is precisely against that residual line that Benhadad's second demand will push, and by pushing it will rouse in Ahab and his elders the desperate resolve that turns the story. For the moment, though, the witnesses leave him where his fear has put him — a king who has given away everything by word, hoping the word will be enough.
5:
The bare notice that 'the messengers came again' carries more weight than its length, and the witnesses read it as the pivot on which Benhadad's insolence turns from demand into outrage. The reformedJohn Gill marks only that they returned 'from Benhadad'; but the return itself is the point, for it means that Ahab's total surrender has not bought peace but invited contempt.

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch supply the psychology the verse implies. Benhadad, they observe, 'was made still more audacious by this submissiveness,' and so 'sent messengers the second time' with a yet heavier notice. The very meekness of Ahab's 'I am thine' has emboldened the aggressor; concession has been read as weakness, and weakness has whetted the appetite it hoped to satisfy. This is the grim logic the second embassy embodies: that there is no bottom to a tyrant's demands until he is resisted.

The puritanMatthew Henry draws the same movement in his running exposition. Ahab had 'laid his all at his feet,' expecting that 'one king would use another generously' and that the mere acknowledgment of Benhadad's sovereignty would content him — 'the honour was sufficient for the present.' But, Henry notes with the Latin tag, Satis est prostrasse leoni, it suffices the lion to have laid his victim prostrate — and Benhadad is no such lion. His 'proud spirit rises upon his submission, and becomes yet more insolent and imperious.' The renewed coming of the messengers is thus the signal that the humbler Ahab grows, the higher his enemy will mount.

The witnesses do not differ here; they agree that this quiet verse teaches a hard truth about the abuse of mercy shown to the merciless. A surrender that hopes to appease only advertises the fear that provokes; and Benhadad, far from lowering his terms upon Ahab's collapse, now frames the demand that will make even a craven king refuse. The second message, which the next verses will spell out, is already implied in these few words — the ambassadors are back at the gate, and their return means that everything Ahab feared has come nearer.
6:
The second demand is the outrage that breaks the surrender, and the witnesses labour over how it differs from the first and what it really intends. The wesleyanAdam Clarke raises the puzzle bluntly: it is 'not easy to discern' how this requisition differs from the first, 'for surely his silver, gold, wives, and children, were among his most pleasant or desirable things.' He records the guess of Jarchi (Rashi) that the coveted object was 'the book of the law of the Lord,' which Benhadad meant to take from Israel; but Clarke judges the plain sense to be worse — that Benhadad 'meant to sack the whole city,' and, having seized the royal treasures and household, 'to deliver up the whole to be pillaged by his soldiers.'

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott agrees that the demand 'is virtually for the plunder of Samaria,' and adds the shrewd point that it 'probably neither expects nor desires acceptance': it is 'a refusal of all but unconditional surrender,' framed to be impossible so that Benhadad may have his war. What the first message claimed as tribute, the second turns into licence to ransack every house at the searchers' discretion — 'whatsoever is pleasant in thine eyes' put into their hands and carried away.

The puritanMatthew Henry unfolds the malice by degrees. Benhadad is now 'as covetous as he is proud,' unwilling to hold the mere title unless he grip the goods; 'as spiteful as he is haughty,' sending servants to insult the prince and strip his palace, and — cruelest touch — ordering them to seize the very things Ahab most dotes upon, for 'we are often crossed in that which we most dote upon, and that proves least safe which is most dear.' He is 'as unreasonable as he is unjust,' construing Ahab's personal surrender as binding on all his subjects, whose houses too shall be searched and plundered. Henry closes with a homely thanksgiving: 'Blessed be God for peace and property, and that what we have we can call our own.' The witnesses do not divide; they agree that this demand, meant to be refused, is the spark that at last strikes fire even from the abject Ahab.
7:
Ahab's summoning of the elders is a small act of returning manhood, and the witnesses use it to expound the ancient institution he falls back on. The reformedJohn Gill notes the change of temper: Ahab's 'poor sneaking spirit was a little aroused with the last message,' and his calling 'a council of the elders of the people upon it' was 'a piece of wisdom in him.' The demand that reached past his own person to the houses of all his subjects has forced him to consult those subjects' representatives.

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott makes the verse an occasion to explain who these 'elders of the land' were. In the last extremity, he observes, Ahab 'falls back on an exceptional appeal to the patriotism of the people.' The elders present in Samaria were the northern kingdom's inheritors of 'the ancient assembly of the elders of Israel, existing from the time of Moses downwards as a senate,' a body 'having power not only of advice, but of concurrence' in relation to judge or king. He traces the institution through the Law and the histories (Exodus 3:16; Exodus 24:1; Deuteronomy 31:9; Joshua 7:6; 2 Samuel 5:3; 1 Kings 8:3), and reads the solemn appointment of the seventy in Numbers 11:24-25 as 'simply the re-constitution and consecration of the original body.' Each tribe and town, he adds, had its lesser elders too (1 Samuel 30:26; Deuteronomy 19:12). Yet the authority of all such assemblies 'must have been at all times largely overborne by the royal power,' varying with time and circumstance — which makes Ahab's appeal to them a mark of his weakness as much as of his wisdom.

The witnesses agree on the sense of the moment. Ahab lays the case before the elders — 'Mark, I pray you, and see how this man seeketh mischief' — confessing that he had already denied Benhadad nothing of his private goods, and now asking counsel in his strait. The very fact that a king so long contemptuous of God's prophets should turn to the elders of his people shows how far he has been driven; and it prepares for their unanimous and bracing answer, which will stiffen his refusal in the verse to come. Behind Ahab's political calculation, the witnesses discern a providence quietly steadying a nation on the edge of ruin.
8:
The elders' counsel is short and brave, and the witnesses read it as the turning of Israel's mind from surrender to defence. The reformedJohn Gill marks their unanimity — 'they were unanimous in their advice' — so that the whole assembly, elders and people together, speak with one voice against yielding.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke gives the reason behind their firmness. 'Hearken not unto him,' they say; and Clarke explains that 'the elders had every thing at stake, and they chose rather to make a desperate defense than tamely to yield to such degrading and ruinous conditions.' The second demand had made the choice clear: to submit was to be plundered house by house, so that resistance, however hopeless it looked, was the only course that left them anything at all.

The puritanMatthew Henry hears in their answer a promise as well as a counsel. They 'speak bravely,' bidding him neither hearken nor consent, and — Henry supposes — 'promising no doubt to stand by him in the refusal.' Their courage supplies what Ahab lacked; a king who could give away his all in a breath is now hemmed about by subjects who will not let him. The contrast is pointed: the crowned head trembles, while the commons of Israel counsel resistance. Clarke's phrase 'degrading and ruinous conditions' captures both grounds of their refusal at once — the shame of vassalage under a plunderer, and the ruin of a city given up to be searched and stripped house by house. Better, they judge, to hazard all in arms than to keep nothing by consenting.

There is no dispute among the witnesses; together they present the elders as the human means by which a craven king is stiffened to do the right and manly thing. Yet the reader is meant to weigh the irony the chapter is building. It is the elders' bravery, not Ahab's faith, that answers Benhadad now; deliverance itself, when it comes, will not be owed to this council or to any strength of Israel's, but to an unnamed prophet and the Lord who sends him. The elders' 'hearken not' is right and needful, but it is only the setting for a mercy that will come from a higher quarter than any senate — the very God whose protection Ahab's sin had forfeited, now stooping to save his undeserving people.
9:
Ahab's answer to Benhadad is a careful refusal, and the witnesses weigh both its firmness and its lingering timidity. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch give the words their exact force: 'All that thou didst send for to thy servant at the first I will do: but this thing I may not do.' Ahab distinguishes the first demand, which he had already granted, from the second, which he now declines; he concedes tribute and vassalage but refuses to open the city to be ransacked at pleasure.

The reformedJohn Gill hears the refusal delivered still 'in a poor sneaking manner after all,' and only 'upon the advice the elders had given him, and encouraged thereby.' The courage is borrowed; Ahab denies Benhadad, but leaning on his council rather than on any strength or faith of his own.

The puritanMatthew Henry reads the same mixture of firmness and deference. Ahab 'expresses himself very modestly in his denial,' still owning Benhadad's dominion over him — 'Tell my lord the king I have no design to affront him' — and standing to what he had already surrendered, while pleading that 'this thing I may not do; I must not give what is none of my own.' Henry catches the political sting for Benhadad: it was 'a mortification' that 'even such an abject spirit as Ahab's durst deny him.' Yet Henry too notes the crutch: 'by his manner of expressing himself,' Ahab 'durst not have done it if his people had not animated him.'

The witnesses agree that the refusal is right but not heroic — a wavering man doing a firm thing because braver men are at his back. It is enough, however, to provoke the aggressor past all patience. Ahab has drawn one line, on the plea that he cannot surrender what belongs to his subjects; and against that single line Benhadad will now hurl the great oath of v.10, boasting the ruin of Samaria to dust. The stage is thus set for the collision the chapter has been preparing, in which the boaster's confidence and the trembler's plea will both be answered — not by either king, but by the word of the Lord.
10:
Benhadad meets the refusal with a great and blustering oath, and the witnesses gather to weigh both its meaning and its emptiness. The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott takes 'the dust of Samaria' as a threat of the city's utter demolition — the dust that will be left 'when razed to the ground' — so that the words carry 'a threat of destruction, as well as a boast of overwhelming strength.' He passes on the 'curious explanation' of Josephus, that if each Syrian took but a handful of dust they could raise a mound against the city higher than its walls; and he paints, with the historian, a Benhadad who receives the message at a feast, 'drinking himself drunk,' too proud to bestir himself and ordering the assault with a single haughty word.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke records the alternative reading of the Targum of Jonathan — 'if the dust of Shomeron shall be sufficient for the soles of the feet of the people that shall accompany me' — that is, 'I shall bring such an army that there will scarcely be room for them to stand in Samaria and its vicinity.' Whether the boast is of destruction or of numbers, its drift is the same: a host so vast that the very ground cannot contain or supply them.

The puritanMatthew Henry hears the oath as impotent rage. Benhadad's swelling wrath, 'meeting with this check,' now 'rage and foam, and make a noise'; in his fury he 'imprecates the impotent revenge of his gods' upon himself if his army cannot reduce Samaria to handfuls of dust. Henry reads the very extravagance as the seed of ruin: 'Thus confident is his pride, thus cruel is his malice; this prepares him to be ruined.'

The reformedJohn Gill keeps the plain sense — the words are addressed 'to Ahab' — and lets the boast stand for what the story will make of it. The witnesses do not contend; they agree that this is the loud confidence God delights to abase, and they set the drunken oath deliberately against the sober proverb Ahab is about to return, so that the reader may see, before a blow is struck, which of the two speaks like a man who knows the uncertainty of war.
11:
Ahab's reply is the one flash of wisdom the witnesses allow him in the whole chapter, and they dwell on it with evident pleasure. The proverb — 'Let not him that girdeth on his harness boast himself as he that putteth it off' — is caught by the grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch in its exact Latin equivalent, ne triumphum canas ante victoriam, sing no triumph before the victory. It is the wisdom of experience: the man buckling on his armour has no right to the boast that belongs only to the man loosing it after the fight is won.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke agrees that this 'was no doubt a proverbial mode of expression,' and cites the Targum of Jonathan to the same effect — 'Let not him who girds himself and goes down to the battle, boast as he who has conquered and returned from it.' The reformedJohn Gill adds only that Ahab answered 'very mildly and very wisely,' declining to match Benhadad's bluster with bluster of his own.

The puritanMatthew Henry makes the most of the saying, calling it 'one of the wisest words that ever Ahab spoke, and a good item or memento to us all.' It is folly, he says, to boast beforehand of any day, 'since we know not what it may bring forth' (Proverbs 27:1), and folly above all to boast of a day of battle. To despise an enemy and count victory sure 'is the way to be beaten.' Then Henry lifts the proverb into the higher warfare: 'Apply it to our spiritual conflicts. Peter fell by his confidence. While we are here we are but girding on the harness, and therefore must never boast as though we had put it off. Happy is the man that feareth always, and is never off his watch.'

The witnesses do not divide; they receive Ahab's answer as sound sense from an unsound man, and note the deeper irony — that the truth Ahab speaks against Benhadad will presently condemn Benhadad, whose triumph-song at the feast comes before any victory at all. The proverb, spoken by a weak king to a proud one, becomes in the witnesses' hands a warning for every soldier of God who would count the battle over while the harness is still being buckled on.
12:
The command to attack is given from a drinking-booth, and the witnesses fix on the folly of a war conducted over the cups. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch explain the setting: the pavilions are succoth, 'booths made of branches, twigs, and shrubs,' still erected in the East for kings and generals in place of tents; and the curt order simu means 'take your places against the city' to storm it — not, as the Septuagint has it, 'build a stockade' or set the siege-train. Adam Clarke adds the etymology, that pavilion comes from papilio, a butterfly, from the look of a tent spread out, and takes simu as a soldier's word of command: 'Begin the attack, Invest the city, Every man to his post.'

The reformedJohn Gill notices the disgrace of the hour — Benhadad and the kings were drinking 'though it was at noon, or before, which shows that he and they were addicted to intemperance.' The puritanMatthew Henry builds a whole meditation on it. Drunkenness, he says, is a sin armies 'have of old been addicted to,' and here it is fatal: 'Had he not been very secure he would not have sat to drink; and, had he not been intoxicated, he would not have been so very secure.' Security and sensuality went together in the old world, as in Sodom in the days of Lot (Luke 17:26); and Benhadad's drunkenness 'was the forerunner of his fall, as Belshazzar's was' (Daniel 5).

Henry sharpens the contrast that governs the whole battle. On the one side the Syrians take their orders 'from a drunken king, who gave orders over his cups,' a man who 'preferred his pleasure before his business' and kept his thirty-two kings drinking with him 'when they should have been at their respective posts to fight for him.' Over such a leader Henry can only cry, 'Woe unto thee, O land, when thy king is such a child.'

The witnesses agree that the verse is the hinge on which the treaty turns to open war, and that the manner of the command foreshadows its outcome. Benhadad orders the assault but does not stir from his booth to see it done; the very self-indulgence that makes him so confident makes him so vulnerable, and the reader is prepared to see a great host undone less by Israel's strength than by its own leader's drunken folly.
13:
Into this scene walks an unnamed prophet, and the witnesses marvel first at the mercy of the errand and then puzzle over the messenger. The reformedJohn Gill reports the Jewish tradition that this was 'Micaiah the son of Imlah,' perhaps one of those Obadiah had hidden in the cave, now sent out publicly to Ahab. The wesleyanAdam Clarke shares the same guess from Jarchi, but confesses the strangeness of the moment: 'It is strange that on such an occasion we hear nothing of Elijah or Elisha. Is it not possible that this was one of them disguised?'

The grammatical-historical voices read the appearance as evidence of a changed climate. Keil and Delitzsch find here no contradiction with the earlier persecutions (1 Kings 18:4); it 'simply shows that the persecution of the prophets by Jezebel had somewhat abated, and therefore Elijah's labour had not remained without fruit.' Charles Ellicott agrees that 'Ahab's enmity to the prophetic order was over since the great day at Carmel,' the schools of the prophets forming themselves again, and notes that Ahab 'receives the prophet's message with perfect confidence and reverence' — for he had returned in profession to the allegiance to Jehovah he had perhaps never wholly relinquished. Ellicott adds the striking observation that in all these political functions of prophecy Elijah does not appear, 'reserving himself for the higher moral and religious mission from God.'

The puritanMatthew Henry presses the wonder of grace. That God should send a prophet 'with a kind and gracious message to so wicked a prince as Ahab' he explains by three motives: for his people Israel's sake, 'the children of the covenant, and not yet cast off'; that he might 'magnify his mercy' toward one so evil and unthankful, either bringing him to repentance or leaving him inexcusable; and that he might 'mortify the pride of Benhadad,' since God resists the proud (Deuteronomy 32:26-27). Henry notes the tender detail that Ahab, who in prosperity could not bear the sight of a prophet, now bids him welcome when the prophets of the groves can give no help — 'he waits to be gracious.'

The witnesses do not quarrel over the prophet's name; they are one in their reading of his message. God foretells a deliverance so unlikely that its coming will prove it 'his own doing,' and appends the purpose that governs the chapter: 'thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' The victory is promised, but it is promised for the honour of God's name, not for the merit of the king who receives it.
14:
Ahab's incredulous 'By whom?' draws out the strangest feature of the promised victory — that it shall be won by the unlikeliest of hands. The reformedJohn Gill reads the question as the doubt of a man who 'had no army with him sufficient to go out with against the Syrian army'; the prophet's answer, that it shall be 'by the young men of the princes of the provinces,' can only deepen his astonishment. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch supply the exchange in full: to Ahab's second question, 'Who shall order the battle?', the prophet answers simply, 'Thou.' The king who has hidden behind his walls must himself lead the sally.

The witnesses work at identifying the 'young men.' Adam Clarke takes them for 'some chosen persons out of the militia of different districts, raised by the princes of the provinces — the same as we would call lord-lieutenants of counties.' Charles Ellicott prefers 'the attendants or armour-bearers of the territorial chiefs, no doubt picked men and well armed.' Either way the force is small, young, and untried, exactly the sort of instrument God delights to use, that the excellency of the power may plainly be of him.

On the second question the grammatical school weighs the Hebrew. Ellicott judges the marginal reading right — not 'who shall order the battle?' but 'Who shall give battle?', 'Who shall begin the fray?' The point is not the marshalling of ranks but the opening of the attack: who shall strike the first blow against so vast a host. And the answer laid on Ahab is 'Thou,' so that the timid king who gave everything away by word must now expose his own person in the field.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the appointment of weakness to confound strength. A handful of young retainers, led by a reluctant king, are named as the sword by which a hundred thousand will be broken — and the naming is itself a sermon. God will not share the glory of the deliverance with the strength of Israel, for Israel has no strength to share; he chooses the few and the feeble on purpose, that when the victory comes there may be no mistaking whose it is.
15:
The muster confirms how desperate the odds are, and the witnesses read the small numbers as the very point of the story. John Gill states the arithmetic with the wonder it deserves: two hundred and thirty-two young men, 'a very small number to go forth against so great an army as the host of the Syrians, and these raw unexperienced young men,' backed by only seven thousand of the people. Charles Ellicott takes the young men for 'the attendants or armour-bearers of the territorial chiefs,' picked and well armed, while the whole garrison of seven thousand was 'enough, perhaps, to man the walls, but wholly unfit to take the field'; and he notes, from Josephus, that the sally was made at noon, 'when the besiegers were resting unarmed in the heat of the day.'

The wesleyanAdam Clarke reads the two hundred and thirty-two as 'the king's life or body guards,' his own regiment, whom the prophet bids him lead in person. Over the seven thousand he exclaims, 'How low must the state of Israel have been at this time!' and passes on the notion of Jarchi that these were the very seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal.

That identification is where the witnesses gently part. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch resist it: the seven thousand here 'are not to be regarded as the 7000' who had not bowed to Baal (1 Kings 19:18), the sameness of the number notwithstanding. The puritanMatthew Henry keeps the coincidence but with the same caution, observing that 'it is the same number with theirs that had not bowed the knee to Baal, though, it is likely, not the same men.'

Beneath that small dispute the witnesses are wholly agreed on the lesson. Henry hears in the choice of 'weak and foolish things' the design of God 'confounding the wise and strong,' so that Benhadad's boasting might be punished and Ahab's own boasting prevented, 'and the excellency of the power may appear to be of God.' The tiny numbered force is not Israel's shame but God's chosen instrument; the fewer the men, the plainer the miracle when the great host melts before them.
16:
The sally begins at midday, and the witnesses set the sober courage of Israel's little band against the drunken helplessness of the Syrian camp. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch catch the deadly detail: 'they went out at noon,' but 'Benhadad was drinking himself drunk in the pavilions, he and the kings, the thirty and two kings that helped him.' The moment chosen for the attack is the very hour the enemy has surrendered to the wine-cup; sobriety marches out against stupor.

The reformedJohn Gill pictures the two hundred and thirty-two young men and the seven thousand issuing from Samaria 'openly, with great spirit and courage, fearless and undaunted.' Yet Gill marks a telling omission: 'it is not said that Ahab went out at the head of them; it seems as if he did not by what is said' — a hesitation in the king that the narrative quietly registers, even after the prophet had bidden him lead.

The contrast the verse draws is the whole burden of the witnesses' reading. On one side a handful of untried youths, going out at the worst hour for a besieged garrison to risk a battle, in the full glare of noon when concealment is impossible; on the other a vast confederate army whose commander and thirty-two royal allies are too far gone in drink to give a coherent order. The odds in numbers all favour Syria; the odds in readiness all favour Israel. K&D underline that this was no chance coincidence of timing but the appointed hour, the thirty-two allied kings carousing 'in the booths' at the very instant the gates of Samaria opened. The one moment when a besieged garrison might least be expected to strike is the moment God chose for it to strike.

The witnesses find in this the fingerprint of providence. The timing that looks so rash — a noonday sortie by the few against the many — is exactly the timing that exposes Benhadad's fatal security, and it is God, through his prophet, who has appointed it. What Benhadad meant as a leisurely siege, punctuated by feasting, becomes the occasion of his rout; and the reader, forewarned by the promise of v.13, watches the little company go out at noon knowing that the drunken confidence in the booths is about to be shattered by the sword of men it had never troubled to fear.
17:
The brief note that the young men 'went out first' is, for the witnesses, the record of the appointed order of the attack — the few before the many, exactly as the prophet had directed. The reformedJohn Gill observes simply that they marched 'from Samaria, before the 7000 did,' the picked retainers leading and the main body following behind.

The verse gains its weight from the Syrian reaction it reports, which the grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch unfold from the surrounding text. Benhadad, informed that 'there are men come out of Samaria,' responds not with alarm but with drunken arrogance, ordering that they be taken alive whether they come for peace or for war. The contrast could hardly be sharper: the young men advance with deadly purpose, while the king who should be marshalling his host treats their sortie as a trifle to be scooped up at leisure.

The witnesses see in this small verse the outworking of the strategy laid down in the prophet's word. The young men are sent out first not by accident but by design, that the blow may fall before the enemy grasps what is happening; and their leading the way is itself an act of faith, for they go out against overwhelming numbers on the strength of a promise, not of any visible advantage. The main force follows to press home what the vanguard begins, but the honour of the first stroke is given to the least likely hands, that the deliverance may plainly be God's.

Read in the light of what follows, the verse marks the instant when the tide begins to turn. The Syrians, secure in their camp and their cups, watch a mere handful emerge and think the day already theirs; but that handful, going out first at the word of the Lord, is the thin edge by which the whole confederacy will be split and scattered. The witnesses let the plainness of the notice stand, content to show how the smallest movements of the story are ordered toward the deliverance God has promised — a deliverance that begins with a few young men stepping out of the gate ahead of everyone else.
18:
Benhadad's order — 'Whether they be come out for peace, take them alive; or whether they be come out for war, take them alive' — becomes for the witnesses a case study in the self-defeating folly of overconfidence. The wesleyanAdam Clarke reads it as sheer presumption: 'He was confident of victory. Do not slay them; bring them to me, they may give us some useful information.' The king does not doubt the outcome for a moment, and so treats the sortie as an opportunity for interrogation rather than a threat to be met.

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch attribute the command to 'his drunken arrogance,' and trace its result: because the order was to seize rather than to kill, the young men 'came out of the city, and the army which followed them,' and 'smote every one his man, so that the Aramaeans fled.' The very leniency of the command opened the Syrians to slaughter, for men sent to capture cannot fight like men sent to kill.

The puritanMatthew Henry makes the point sharpest of all. To take the assailants alive 'might have been done more easily and safely' by killing them; to insist on seizing them 'gave them an opportunity of killing the aggressors.' The order was both imprudent and unjust — unjust, because it would have made prisoners of men who, for all Benhadad knew, came to renew the treaty in peace. 'Thus, as is usual, he drinks, and forgets the law, both the policies and the justice of war.'

The witnesses stand together on the moral: pride and drink between them have unmanned the Syrian command. The same overweening confidence that would not stir from the booth now gives an order that turns the besiegers into targets. Benhadad's word, meant to display his mastery, instead exposes his whole army; and the reader sees, in this single sentence of a drunken king, how God 'takes away the spirit of princes' — using the enemy's own arrogance as the instrument of his overthrow, so that the boast of the feast is answered by disaster in the field.
19:
The verse marks the moment of contact, as vanguard and main body close with the enemy, and the witnesses read it as the quiet execution of the prophet's plan. The reformedJohn Gill notes only that 'these young men of the princes of the provinces came out of the city' and 'marched forwards towards the Syrian camp' — the picked retainers in front, the seven thousand of the host behind them.

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch fill in the formation from the surrounding narrative: 'the servants of the governors at the head, and the rest of the army behind.' The order of march is not incidental; it is the shape God gave the sally through his prophet — the few going first to strike, the many following to press the advantage home. What Benhadad's scouts had reported as a mere trickle of men from the gate is in fact the whole fighting strength of Samaria, moving in deliberate order against a camp that has not troubled to arm.

The witnesses treat the verse as the pause before the blow, the instant when the two forces are about to meet and everything hangs on the promise given in v.13. On every visible measure the encounter should be hopeless: a handful of untrained youths and a small garrison advancing on a confederate army that 'filled the country.' Yet they advance, and their advance is itself the obedience of faith — going forward on the strength of a word, into the teeth of odds that reason would call suicidal.

Read against what immediately follows, the verse is the last still moment of the story before the rout. The young men and the army that follows them are stepping into the space between the promise and its fulfilment; and the witnesses leave them there, marching forward, so that the reader may feel the weight of the venture before the sword falls. In a breath the Syrians will be fleeing and the great host melting away, but here, for an instant, all that can be seen is a small company of Israelites walking toward an enemy far too strong for them — trusting that the God who bade them go has already given the victory into their hand.
20:
The blow falls and the great host breaks, and the witnesses gather to describe the rout and to draw its lesson. The reformedJohn Gill records that 'they slew every one his man,' a body of Syrians meeting them being cut down, and reckons the first slaughter at the number of the attackers themselves. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch carry it further: 'the Syrians fled; and Israel pursued them: and Benhadad the king of Syria escaped on an horse with the horsemen' — the boaster of the feast now galloping for his life, saved only by his cavalry.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke supplies the cause in a phrase: 'The Syrians fled — they were doubtless panic-struck.' A camp taken unready, its commander drunk and its order dissolved, needed no miracle to shatter it; terror did the work. On this the grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott agrees, comparing the sudden collapse to the rout of the Philistine garrison in the days of Saul (1 Samuel 14) and the earlier scattering of Midian by Gideon's night attack (Judges 7:16-23). 'It does not necessarily follow,' Ellicott cautions, 'that the event was miraculous,' for 'such dispersions of vast Oriental armies are not uncommon in history'; yet he draws the same theological lesson Jonathan drew, 'with noble simplicity' — 'There is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few' (1 Samuel 14:6).

The puritanMatthew Henry lets the reversal preach. 'The proud Syrians were beaten, and the poor despised Israelites were more than conquerors.' Benhadad, who had despised the little band, now 'durst not face them, but mounted immediately, drunk as he was, and made the best of his way.' Henry cannot resist the taunt: 'Now where are the silver and gold he demanded of Ahab? Where are the handfuls of Samaria's dust?' And he draws the settled truth: 'See how God takes away the spirit of princes, and makes himself terrible to the kings of the earth.'

Whether they call the collapse a plain rout or the finger of God — and Ellicott, unlike Henry, is careful not to insist on miracle — the witnesses land on one confession. The great host was broken by a few, so that no flesh might glory; and the man who swore by his gods to grind Samaria to dust is left fleeing on horseback, his boast and his army alike undone.
21:
The victory is pressed home, and the witnesses read the king's belated advance and the great slaughter as the completion of a deliverance that was never Ahab's to claim. The reformedJohn Gill pictures the moment: 'when he saw the Syrians fleeing, and his army pursuing, he went forth' from Samaria, 'perhaps with more forces, who were now willing to join with him'; and he passes on the note of Josephus that 'Ahab had another army within the walls,' held in reserve until the rout made it safe to commit. The king who would not lead the sally is quick enough to share the pursuit.

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch render the tactical result plainly: 'the king of Israel went out, and smote the horses and chariots, and slew the Syrians with a great slaughter.' The very arms in which Syria trusted — the cavalry and chariotry that made the plain their natural element — are turned to carnage once the ranks are broken; horses and chariots, unready for the sudden sally, become a liability rather than a strength.

The puritanMatthew Henry weighs the mercy shown to so undeserving a king. 'Ahab failed not to improve this advantage, but slew the Syrians with a great slaughter'; and Henry lays down the principle the whole episode illustrates: 'God oftentimes makes one wicked man a scourge to another.' The idolater Ahab is the rod by which the blasphemer Benhadad is beaten — neither of them righteous, but the one made, for a season, the instrument of the other's chastisement.

The witnesses agree that the verse closes the first campaign on a note of paradox. A wicked king triumphs; a persecutor conquers; and yet the glory belongs to neither, for the victory was promised and given by God, that Ahab might 'know that I am the LORD.' The horses and chariots lie broken on the field, the great host is scattered, and Benhadad is fled — but the lesson the witnesses take is not Ahab's prowess. It is that the Lord, for his own name's sake and his people's, can use even an unworthy hand to abase the proud, and that the deliverance which crowns this day is wholly of grace and not at all of desert.
22:
The victory is scarcely won before the prophet returns to warn of the next assault, and the witnesses read the verse as a lesson in vigilance after mercy. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time. Keil and Delitzsch render the prophet's counsel — 'Go, strengthen thyself, and mark, and see what thou doest' — and explain that 'at the return of the year,' i.e. the next spring (2 Samuel 11:1), the Syrian king would renew the war. Charles Ellicott agrees, glossing it as 'the early part of the next year, after the winter was over, when kings go out to battle.'

The reformedJohn Gill notes that this is 'the same prophet that came to him before,' so that the word of warning comes from the same quarter as the word of promise. The puritanMatthew Henry makes the pastoral application. Ahab, he supposes, 'was now secure, and looked but a little way before him'; and 'those that are careless of their souls are often as careless of their outward affairs.' The prophet, to whom God had made known the very counsels of the Syrians, bids him strengthen himself and be ready — 'God had decreed the end, but Ahab must use the means, else he tempts God.' Henry compresses it into a maxim: 'Help thyself, strengthen thyself, and God will help and strengthen thee.'

Henry widens the warning into a truth about all the church's foes. 'The enemies of God's Israel are restless in their malice, and, though they may take some breathing-time for themselves, yet they are still breathing out threatenings and slaughter against the church.' It concerns us, therefore, 'always to expect assaults from our spiritual enemies, and therefore to mark and see what we do.' The Syrians will indeed return, hoping 'to retrieve the honour they had lost and be avenged for the blow they had received.'

The witnesses agree that grace received is no ground for slackness. The same God who gave the first deliverance forewarns of the second, and requires his people to prepare even for a battle he has already resolved to win. Providence and prudence are not rivals; the promise of victory is the very reason to muster and watch, so that Ahab, and every believer after him, must gird for the fight precisely because the outcome rests in the hand of the God who bids him get ready.
23:
Benhadad's counsellors trace the defeat to theology and geography, and the witnesses expose the ignorance in their advice while conceding its shrewdness. Their claim — 'Their gods are gods of the hills; therefore they were stronger than we; but let us fight against them in the plain' — is set by the grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch in its pagan frame: the Syrians reasoned that the God of Israel was a mountain deity because his sacred places, the temple on Moriah and the altars of the high places, 'were erected upon mountains,' and supposed his power would fail on the plain. Charles Ellicott agrees that 'the idea of tutelary gods, whose strength was greatest on their own soil, is naturally common in polytheistic religions,' and notes that Israel, having little cavalry, 'naturally encamped and fought, as far as possible, on the hills.' Yet he detects policy under the superstition: 'shrewd policy might lurk in the advice,' both in choosing the plain where chariots and horsemen fight at advantage and in the accompanying scheme 'to increase the central power' by replacing the vassal-kings with the king's own officers.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke reads the same double motive. The small Israelite army had likely 'availed itself of the heights and uneven ground' to blunt the Syrian cavalry, whose chariots were 'nearly useless' there; on the plain 'our horses and chariots will all be able to bear on the enemy.' Behind the tactics lay the creed: 'it was a general belief in the heathen world that each district had its tutelary and protecting deity, who could do nothing out of his own sphere.'

The puritanMatthew Henry turns the counsel into an anatomy of unbelief. The Syrians 'take it for granted that it was not Israel, but Israel's gods, that beat them,' and yet 'speak very ignorantly of Jehovah' at every point — that he was many, when he is one; that he was a local deity, when he is the world's Creator; that he was a God of the hills only, perhaps because their prophet had sung, 'I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills, whence cometh my help' (Ps. 121:1). 'Thus vain were the Gentiles in their imaginations concerning God,' Henry concludes, professing themselves wise while their foolish hearts were darkened.

The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the counsel mixed sound generalship with blind blasphemy. And they mark the trap the Syrians are setting for themselves: by confining the Lord to the hills they have named the very ground on which he will most signally prove himself God of the valleys too, so that their shrewd advice becomes the occasion of their greater ruin.
24:
The second piece of Syrian counsel is a reorganization of the command, and the witnesses weigh both its worldly wisdom and its place in the story. The advice — 'Take the kings away, every man out of his place, and put captains in their rooms' — is read by the wesleyanAdam Clarke as sound generalship: the thirty-two kings 'were not acquainted with military affairs, or they had not competent skill,' and Benhadad is urged to 'put experienced captains in their place, and fight not but on the plains, and you will be sure of victory.'

The puritanMatthew Henry draws out the principle. Benhadad is counselled to employ 'not the kings, who were commanders by birth, but captains rather, who were commanders by merit, who were inured to war' and 'would go through with business.' Henry finds in it a general rule of good order: 'Let every man be employed in that which he is brought up to and used to, and preferred to that which he is fit for.' He notes too the resilience it implies — that Syria was so 'rich and populous' that, after so crushing a defeat, it could furnish recruits enough for a fresh army, 'horse for horse, chariot for chariot.'

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch take the occasion to locate the coming battlefield. Benhadad 'went up to Aphek again to fight against Israel,' and this Aphek, they argue, is 'neither the city of that name in the tribe of Asher, nor that on the mountains of Judah,' but 'the city in the plain of Jezreel not far from Endor' (1 Samuel 29:1), 'since Benhadad had resolved that this time he would fight against Israel in the plain.' The choice of ground follows directly from the counsellors' theology of the valleys.

The witnesses agree that the advice was, on its own terms, excellent — and that this is precisely the pathos of it. Everything human prudence could contrive was contrived: better ground, better officers, an army rebuilt to full strength. Yet all of it was reared upon a blasphemous premise, that the God of Israel could be outflanked by choosing the plain; and the witnesses let the reader see that the more carefully the Syrians prepare, the more completely their defeat will vindicate the God they have miscalculated. Sound strategy in the service of a false creed is only ruin better organized.
25:
The counsellors complete their plan by bidding Benhadad rebuild his forces to their old strength, and the witnesses read the verse as the measure of Syria's resources and of the confidence resting on them. The reformedJohn Gill notes the boldness of the advice — 'Raise an army of an equal number, which they supposed he was able to do' — so that the very demand assumes a kingdom able to replace a shattered host at will.

The words spell out the ambition: 'number thee an army, like the army that thou hast lost, horse for horse, and chariot for chariot: and we will fight against them in the plain, and surely we shall be stronger than they.' The puritanMatthew Henry, expounding this same counsel, marvels that Syria could furnish 'recruits sufficient, after so great a defeat, horse for horse, chariot for chariot' — evidence of how 'rich and populous' the kingdom was. The plan is not merely to try again but to try again with every advantage doubled: the same numbers, the better officers, the chosen ground.

The witnesses hear in the closing boast — 'surely we shall be stronger than they' — the same confident presumption that had already been rebuked. It is the calculation of men who have learned everything from their defeat except the one thing that caused it. They have corrected their tactics and their command; they have not corrected their theology. Having reasoned that Israel's God was a God of the hills, they set about assembling, on the plain, a force so overwhelming that no God, as they imagine, could stand against it.

Read against what follows, the verse is the last stroke of human preparation before the divine reply. Everything is now in place for a Syrian triumph on paper — full numbers, seasoned captains, level ground — and the very completeness of the arrangement heightens the wonder of its overthrow. The witnesses let the boast stand unanswered for the moment, knowing that the God whom the Syrians have written off will presently deliver 'all this great multitude' into the hand of a people 'like two little flocks of kids,' so that the more strength Benhadad gathers, the more signal will be the demonstration that the battle is the Lord's.
26:
The new campaign opens at Aphek, and the witnesses spend the verse chiefly on locating the place, a question on which they frankly differ. The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott observes that the name 'Aphek' means simply 'a fortress,' and so 'is applied to several different places.' Two suit this battlefield in the plain country between Israel and Syria: 'the Aphek of 1 Samuel 29:1, evidently in the plain of Esdraelon,' or 'a place on the road to Damascus, about six miles east of the Sea of Galilee.'

The wesleyanAdam Clarke ventures a quite different site, placing this Aphek far to the north, 'in Libanus, upon the river Adonis, between Heliopolis and Biblos.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch, for their part, argue for 'the city in the plain of Jezreel not far from Endor,' precisely because Benhadad 'had resolved that this time he would fight against Israel in the plain.' The reformedJohn Gill is content to note the season, 'at the return of the year, in the spring.'

The witnesses agree that the geography, however disputed, serves one narrative purpose: Benhadad has deliberately sought the level ground his counsellors advised, the very terrain on which he means to prove that the God of Israel cannot save. The choice of a fortress-town in the plain is the strategy of v.23 made concrete. Everything about the second campaign is engineered to reverse the conditions of the first — no more hills, no more cramped approaches, no more amateur royal commanders.

Beneath the differing maps, then, the expositors find a single point of agreement more important than the precise dot on it. Wherever Aphek lay, Benhadad has come there on purpose, confident that on such a field the arithmetic of horses and chariots must decide the day. The witnesses let his confidence stand, reserving their comment for the man of God who will meet Ahab on the same plain and turn the Syrians' own boast — 'the LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys' — into the reason for their destruction. The place-name that opens the campaign is, in the witnesses' hands, already heavy with the irony the chapter is about to spring.
27:
The two armies face each other in ludicrous disproportion, and the witnesses dwell on the image the text supplies — Israel 'like two little flocks of kids,' the Syrians filling the land. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch render the picture exactly: the Israelites, 'mustered and provided for,' encamped before the enemy 'like two little separate flocks of goats' — that is, severed from the great herd — 'in contrast with the Syrians who filled the land.' They read the Hebrew kilkelu as 'supplied with ammunition and provisions,' so that Israel came not unprepared but simply, pitiably few.

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott confirms that reading of the difficult clause 'were all present,' preferring the margin, 'were victualled,' or more generally 'were supplied with all things necessary for war.' And he draws a sober historical inference from the smallness of the force even after the previous year's great victory: it 'appears to show that, previous to the siege of Samaria, Ahab had suffered some great defeats, which had broken the strength of Israel.'

The puritanMatthew Henry lets the contrast preach. The children of Israel, 'cantoned in two battalions, looked like two little flocks of kids — their numbers small, their equipage mean, and the figure they made contemptible'; while the Syrians 'filled the country with their numbers, their noise, their chariots, their carriages, and their baggage.' The comparison is not accidental: kids are the feeblest of the flock, defenceless and easily scattered, and that is precisely how Israel appeared drawn up on the slopes above the plain.

The witnesses are one in seeing the disproportion as the point. God has again arranged that his people should look utterly overmatched, that when the deliverance comes it may be seen to be his and not theirs. The 'two little flocks of kids' are the visible pledge of the invisible truth the man of God is about to announce — that the multitude which fills the country will be given into the hand of the few, not because the few are strong, but because the Lord will not have his name blasphemed as a God confined to the hills. The meaner the figure Israel makes, the louder the coming victory will speak.
28:
The man of God announces the victory, and the witnesses fasten on the reason he gives — not Ahab's worth but the Syrians' blasphemy. The word is plain: 'Because the Syrians have said, The LORD is God of the hills, but he is not God of the valleys, therefore will I deliver all this great multitude into thine hand.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch read it as the deliberate answer to the counsel of v.23: the God whom the Syrians confined to the mountains will vindicate himself on the plain, 'that ye may know that I am Jehovah.'

The wesleyanAdam Clarke puts the motive bluntly: 'God resents their blasphemy, and is determined to punish it. They shall now be discomfited in such a way as to show that God's power is every where, and that the multitude of a host is nothing against him.' The puritanMatthew Henry presses the same point with a warning against any thought that Ahab deserved the mercy. God 'would not do it because Ahab had praised God or prayed to him' — 'we do not read that he did either' — 'but because the Syrians had blasphemed God.' Had the Syrians only presumed on Israel's apostasy, Henry reasons, God might well have given his people up; but because they impugned 'the divine omnipotence' itself, 'they shall be undeceived, at the expense of that vast army which is so much their pride and confidence.'

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott thinks this a 'man of God' distinct from the earlier prophet — the prophetic order being now numerous — and sets the vindication of God's majesty before the heathen alongside the later rebuke of Sennacherib's blasphemy (2 Kings 19:16-34). He hears in it 'a foreshadowing of that view of all nations, as in some degree having knowledge of God and probation before Him,' worked out later in the prophets (Ezekiel 20:9).

The witnesses are united in the lesson. The coming slaughter is an act of self-vindication by the living God, jealous for his name among the nations; the deliverance falls on Israel, but it is aimed at Syria's false creed. And in that the witnesses find comfort even for an unworthy people: when God will not act for our sake, he may yet act for his own name's sake, and the honour of that name is a surer ground of hope than any merit of ours.
29:
After a week of waiting the battle is joined, and the witnesses record a slaughter so vast it strains belief. The reformedJohn Gill pictures the stand-off: 'very probably the Israelites pitched upon an hill,' and the Syrians, though so greatly superior, waited for them to shift ground before attacking. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch give the outcome: 'in the seventh day the battle was fought,' and 'the children of Israel slew of the Syrians an hundred thousand footmen in one day.'

The number staggers the commentators. The wesleyanAdam Clarke admits, 'This number is enormous; but the MSS. and versions give no various reading' — the text is firm, however hard it presses on the imagination. Keil and Delitzsch reach for a patristic gloss, quoting Theodoret's comment that the disaster fell out 'that both these and those might learn' the plague was heaven-sent — that is, that Syrian and Israelite alike might read in it the hand of God, and not the mere fortune of war.

The witnesses are agreed that the seven days' delay is itself significant. It gave time for the Syrians' confidence to swell and, as Henry elsewhere imagines, for the Israelites to tremble; and it made unmistakable, when the blow at last fell, that the victory was no accident of a surprise sally like the first, but a set battle on the enemy's own chosen plain. On the very ground where the Syrians had staked their creed — that the valleys were beyond the reach of Israel's God — a hundred thousand of them fell in a single day.

The reading the witnesses take is the one the man of God had announced: this is God vindicating his name. The immensity of the number is not an embarrassment to be explained away but the scale of the demonstration, matching the greatness of the blasphemy it answers. A host that 'filled the country' is mown down before 'two little flocks of kids,' so that both nations should know, as Theodoret says, whose stroke it was — and the survivors, fleeing to Aphek, will find that even the city walls afford no shelter from the God they thought they had outmanoeuvred.
30:
The rout is completed by a falling wall, and the witnesses divide over its cause even as they agree on its meaning. The text is stark: the survivors 'fled to Aphek, into the city; and there a wall fell upon twenty and seven thousand of the men that were left. And Benhadad fled, and came into the city, into an inner chamber.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch read the collapse as 'the special interposition of God': the fleeing Syrians had crowded to the wall, some to defend it and some to shelter behind it, and 'during the conflict the wall fell and buried' them. They note pointedly that Thenius, 'in order to remove all idea of any miraculous working of the omnipotence of God,' assumes the wall was merely undermined.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke canvasses the natural explanations and hints at another. On the surface it seems Israel 'sapped the walls,' so that a large portion fell and buried the twenty-seven thousand; but he suspects 'the hand of God was more immediately in this disaster,' and floats the theory he will argue at length on v.43 — that the word rendered 'wall' may denote a burning, blasting wind. The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott leaves the mechanism open — the wall falling 'whether by earthquake, or in the storming of the place' — and cautions, as often, that the very large numbers 'may' reflect textual corruption, though 'the same numbers are found in the ancient versions.'

The puritanMatthew Henry inclines to earthquake, and preaches the reversal. The twenty-seven thousand 'found their bane where they hoped for protection'; the wall, 'probably overthrown by an earthquake,' crushed the men who fled to it. And Benhadad, who had thought his fortress secure, 'hid himself in a chamber within a chamber' — so that 'the greatest confidence often ends in the greatest cowardice.' Henry cannot resist recalling the taunt of v.23: 'Now is the God of Israel the God of the valleys or no?'

Whether they name earthquake, siege-work, or a scorching wind, the witnesses close ranks on the theology. The wall that fell on the very men who ran to it for safety is God's answer to the creed of the plains; the survivors are undone by the ground they had chosen, and the great king who dictated terms to Ahab is reduced to hiding in an inner room. The debate over the means only underscores the agreement over the Author.
31:
Benhadad's courtiers counsel surrender on the strength of Israel's reputation for mercy, and the witnesses read the scene both as history and as a parable of the sinner's approach to God. The proposal is exact: 'we have heard that the kings of the house of Israel are merciful kings; let us put sackcloth on our loins, and ropes upon our heads, and go out to the king of Israel.' The reformedJohn Gill notes their desperation — the wall fallen, escape impossible, concealment brief — so that throwing themselves on Ahab's clemency was the only hope left.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke and the grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott both hear in the sackcloth and halters the language of abject, unconditional surrender, and both recall the six burghers of Calais, who in 1346 came out to Edward III with ropes about their necks to sue for their city — 'naked, helpless criminals,' in Ellicott's phrase, offering themselves to a conqueror's mercy.

The puritanMatthew Henry turns the whole verse to gospel. The kings of Israel were reputed merciful, 'not oppressive to their subjects,' and, Henry suggests, this because 'the God of Israel proclaimed his name gracious and merciful, and they concluded their kings would make their God their pattern.' From that he draws the encouragement 'poor sinners have to repent and humble themselves before God': 'Have we not heard that the God of Israel is a merciful God? Have we not found him so? Let us therefore rend our hearts and return to him' (Joel 2:13). That, Henry says, 'is evangelical repentance which flows from an apprehension of the mercy of God in Christ; there is forgiveness with him.'

The witnesses do not divide; they take the Syrians' calculated humility as a true picture of how mercy emboldens the guilty to come. Yet the story will not let the parallel run smooth, for the mercy Ahab is about to show is the wrong mercy at the wrong time — a sparing God had forbidden. So the witnesses hold two things together: that the sinner may indeed venture on divine mercy without measure, and that a king may sin grievously by extending, in God's name, a clemency God never authorized.
32:
The suppliants come, and Ahab's first word undoes the victory God had given. To their plea, 'Thy servant Benhadad saith, I pray thee, let me live,' the king answers, 'Is he yet alive? he is my brother.' The wesleyanAdam Clarke marks the whirl of fortune: 'A little before he was the haughtiest of all tyrants, and Ahab calls him his lord; now, so much is he humbled, that he will be glad to be reputed Ahab's slave.'

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch lay bare Ahab's motive and its guilt. He 'felt so flattered by the fact that his powerful opponent was obliged to come and entreat his favour in this humble manner, that he gave him his life' — and this, they add, 'without considering how a similar act on the part of Saul had been blamed' by the prophet in an earlier day. Vanity, not clemency, opens Ahab's mouth.

The puritanMatthew Henry presses the same charge. Ahab was 'proud to be thus courted by him whom he had feared,' and enquired for him 'with great tenderness: Is he yet alive? He is my brother, brother-king, though not brother-Israelite.' Henry sees the flaw exposed: Ahab 'valued himself more upon his royalty than on his religion, and others accordingly.' He puts the reproach into a series of questions: 'Did he use thee like a brother when he sent thee that barbarous message? Would he have called thee brother if he had been the conqueror? Would he now have called himself thy servant if he had not been reduced to the utmost strait?'

The reformedJohn Gill keeps the plain sense — the Syrians came owning themselves captives, ropes on their heads, ready to be hanged if he so pleased. The witnesses do not differ; they agree that Ahab's 'he is my brother' is not magnanimity but self-flattery, and that the fatal word betrays a king who feels the honour of being begged more than the charge of executing God's sentence. Where God had marked Benhadad for destruction, Ahab hears only a fellow-monarch calling him great — and by that vanity the whole tragedy of the chapter's close is set in motion.
33:
The Syrian envoys pounce on Ahab's careless word, and the witnesses read the moment as a study in how a hasty royal phrase becomes a binding pledge. The reformedJohn Gill catches the eager watching: the men 'diligently observe whether anything would come from him' that might be 'a good omen to them,' watching Ahab 'as soothsayers do when they look out for a lucky sign' — for the word, he notes, is sometimes used of divining.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke agrees that they 'were watching to see if any kind word should be spoken by him, from which they might draw a favorable omen; and when they heard him use the word brother, it gave them much encouragement.' The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott renders the graphic scene most fully: 'The men watched, and hasted, and caught up what fell from him' — 'as for augury,' says the Septuagint — 'so as to make it sure.' The Syrians speak of 'thy slave Benhadad'; Ahab, 'in compassion or show of magnanimity, says, my brother'; and 'eagerly the ambassadors catch up the word, which, according to Eastern custom, implied a pledge of amity not to be recalled.' Ahab then 'accepts their inference, and seals it publicly by taking the conquered king into his chariot' (compare 2 Kings 10:15-16).

The witnesses see in this the swift closing of a trap Ahab has sprung on himself. A word dropped in vanity is seized as an oath; the courtiers, quicker than the king, fasten on 'brother' before he can qualify it, and by their eager acceptance make retreat impossible. To seal the amity Ahab does the astonishing thing of lifting his beaten enemy up beside him in the royal chariot — an honour that publicly ratifies the pardon.

Beneath the diplomacy the witnesses discern the deeper error. Ahab is being managed by the men he had conquered, led by their flattery to bind himself to the very course God had forbidden. The scene is all courtesy and quickness on the surface; underneath, a king who cannot govern his own tongue is handing back, with his own hand, the enemy the Lord had given into it — and turning a victory into the occasion of his doom.
34:
The pardon is formalized in a treaty, and the witnesses examine both its terms and its rashness. Benhadad offers to restore 'the cities which my father took from thy father' and to grant Ahab 'streets in Damascus, as my father made in Samaria'; Ahab replies, 'I will send thee away with this covenant,' and does so. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch unravel the history behind the 'streets,' arguing that it points to a war between Omri and the first Benhadad, in which the Syrian had won the right to trading quarters in Samaria itself.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke explains these 'streets' as foreign quarters where merchants might 'deposit and vend their merchandise' — 'the very origin of European settlements in Asiatic countries,' where a trading concession bred forts and at last dominion. The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott calls them properly 'squares, or quarters of a city,' and reads the concession as 'a virtual acknowledgment of supremacy,' since such rights in a king's capital belong only to his overlord. But Ellicott's chief note is on the manner of the peace: 'the covenant was made hastily, on insufficient security,' and 'the great point was that a war, victoriously conducted under prophetic guidance, should not have been concluded without prophetic sanction.'

The puritanMatthew Henry is severest. Ahab made the covenant 'not consulting God's prophets, or the elders of the land, or himself, concerning what was fit to be insisted on,' but let Benhadad, 'as if he had been conqueror, make his own terms.' He might have demanded cities, treasures, magazines; he 'was content with a poor liberty to build streets there, a point of honour and no advantage,' and sent the blasphemer away 'without so much as reproving him for his blasphemous reflections upon the God of Israel.' Henry lays down the verdict: 'There are those on whom success is ill bestowed; let favour be shown to the wicked, yet will he not learn righteousness.'

The reformedJohn Gill adds a textual observation, following Osiander, that the words of concession in this verse read best as Ahab's own speech to Benhadad rather than Benhadad's to Ahab — the victor, in his 'great and superabundant kindness,' dictating even the enemy's reparations for him. The witnesses stand together: whatever the exact terms or the exact speaker, the treaty was a presumptuous peace, struck without God, that gave life and honour to the man God had doomed.
35:
The scene shifts to the prophets, and the witnesses read the strange command 'Smite me' as the opening of a divine indictment against Ahab's mercy. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch explain the method: a disciple of the prophets, charged 'in the word of Jehovah' to announce that God would punish the king for releasing Benhadad, prepares to do it as Nathan once did with David (2 Samuel 12:1) — 'by a symbolical action, whereby the king was led to pronounce sentence upon himself.' When his companion refuses to strike him, the prophet foretells that a lion will kill him for disobeying the voice of the Lord.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke underlines the ground of the coming judgment: the command was 'by the word or command of the Lord,' so that in refusing to smite, the man 'forfeited his life.' The reformedJohn Gill passes on the tradition that this prophet was Micaiah, the more likely because Ahab later recognizes him when the disguise is removed.

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott pauses on the phrase itself, for 'the sons of the prophets' first appears here. He explains that 'sonship' means not descent but discipleship — the prophetic office 'seems never to have been hereditary,' as Amos protested, 'I was no prophet, neither was I a prophet's son' (Amos 7:14) — and that these schools were 'places of higher religious education,' probably revived, after long disuse, 'to seal and to secure the prophetic victory over Baal-worship.'

The witnesses agree that everything here is deliberate and divinely ordered, however odd it looks. The wound the prophet seeks is not self-mutilation but a costume for a parable, and the command to inflict it is a real command of God, obedience to which is a matter of life and death. Even before the parable reaches Ahab, the lesson is being enacted: the word of the Lord is not to be trifled with, and the disobedience of the sparing king will be measured against the disobedience of a prophet's friend who would not strike when God said strike. The very oddity of the command — that a man should ask to be struck and wounded — is meant to arrest, and the reader who is tempted to think the demand harsh is being prepared to judge more harshly still the king who thought God's ban on Benhadad might be set aside for a compliment.
36:
The refusal is punished at once, and the witnesses read the lion that slays the disobedient prophet as a terrible pledge of what awaits Ahab. The sentence is exact: 'Because thou hast not obeyed the voice of the LORD, behold, as soon as thou art departed from me, a lion shall slay thee'; and so it fell out. The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch read the episode as a demonstration 'with how severe a punishment all opposition to the commandments' of God is visited, even in his own servants.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke feels the harshness but justifies it: 'This seems a hard measure, but there was ample reason for it.' The man was himself a son of the prophets and 'knew that God frequently delivered his counsels in this way, and should have immediately obeyed'; the smiting 'could have had no evil in it when God commanded it.' The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott simply points to the parallel of the disobedient prophet slain by a lion in an earlier chapter (1 Kings 13:24), while the reformedJohn Gill notes that a prophet, of all men, was 'inexcusable' in disregarding what was said in the Lord's name.

The puritanMatthew Henry draws the argument the whole sign is meant to carry. If a good prophet was so punished 'for sparing his friend and God's, when God said, Smite,' then 'of much sorer punishment should a wicked king be thought worthy, who spared his enemy and God's, when God said, Smite.' Henry turns it into a rebuke of Ahab's very clemency: 'Shall mortal man pretend to be more just than God, more pure or more compassionate than his Maker? We must be merciful as he is merciful, and not otherwise.'

The witnesses do not soften the lesson. The lion is not cruelty but consistency: the same God who forbids his prophets to disobey forbids his kings to overrule his sentence, and the fate of the friend who would not strike is the living warning to the king who would not slay. Mercy that sets itself against God's declared judgment is not mercy but rebellion; and the reader, watching the lion, already knows what the disguised prophet will presently say to Ahab's face.
37:
The prophet seeks a second man to wound him, and the witnesses read the fulfilled request as the completing of his costume and the sealing of his object-lesson. Where the first man had refused and paid with his life, this one obeys: 'the man smote him, so that in smiting he wounded him.' The reformedJohn Gill marks the difference between the two — the second is 'not said to be his neighbour, nor one of the sons of the prophets, but a rustic man, and so stuck not to take him at his word.'
