The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch render the doubled Hebrew precisely — 'smiting and wounding,' so that the blow not only struck but drew blood — and explain the purpose the wound was to serve. The prophet did not seek it to disguise himself merely, nor to feign a returning soldier's hurt, but 'to show to Ahab symbolically what he had to expect from Benhadad whom he had released.' The self-inflicted wound is a prophecy in the flesh: the harm Ahab has stored up for himself by his misplaced mercy.

The witnesses find in the contrast of the two men a quiet commentary on obedience. The first, a fellow-prophet who should have known better, hesitated over a command that seemed strange and cruel, and was destroyed; the second, an ordinary countryman with no scruple, simply did as he was asked. It is a pointed reversal — the initiate stumbling where the layman obeys — and it drives home again the theme of the whole passage, that the word of the Lord is to be done, not weighed.

The verse, brief as it is, moves the drama to its threshold. The prophet now bears on his own body the sign he means to press upon the king: a real wound, honestly got, that will lend his parable the look of a soldier's grievance. He is ready to take his station by the road and wait for Ahab to pass. All that has gone before — the refused blow, the lion, the second and effectual stroke — has been preparation for the single sentence the king will shortly pronounce, all unknowing, against himself.
38:
The prophet takes his post by the road in disguise, and the witnesses spend the verse largely on the nature of that disguise. The reformedJohn Gill sets the scene: the prophet 'waited for the king by the way, as he went from Aphek to Samaria, his royal seat.' The disguise itself, the text says, was made 'with ashes upon his face' — but here the grammatical commentators demur.

The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch insist the word does 'not mean ashes' but answers to a 'head-band,' a bandage, matching the Septuagint's telamon; the prophet 'disguised himself by a bandage over his eyes.' The wesleyanAdam Clarke reaches the same conclusion by a different road, following Houbigant's conjecture that the Hebrew should read not apher, dust, but aphad, a fillet or bandage — the two words differing only by the corner of a single letter — and finding the reading confirmed in 'the two of the oldest versions, the Septuagint and the Chaldee,' which render 'he bound his eyes with a fillet' and 'he covered his eyes with a cloth.' The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott agrees it should be 'a bandage over his head, to cover his face, and to accord with the appearance of a wounded soldier.'

Ellicott adds the question the disguise raises about the wound itself: 'Unless the wound had some symbolic significance in application to Ahab or Israel, it is difficult to see what purpose it could serve' — pointing back to the reading that the injury was itself part of the message, not mere stage-dressing.

The witnesses agree on the essential picture, whether they call the covering ashes or a bandage: a man got up to look like a soldier hurt in the recent battle, his face hidden so the king will not know him, standing at the roadside to intercept Ahab on his way home. The elaborate preparation — the wound, the disguise, the chosen station — is all in the service of the trap Nathan-like about to be sprung. The prophet has made himself unrecognizable precisely so that Ahab will judge the case on its merits and, judging it, condemn himself before he learns whose case it is.
39:
The prophet accosts the passing king with a fictitious tale, and the witnesses recognize at once the Nathan-like snare it lays. The story is circumstantial: a prisoner was committed to the speaker's charge in the heat of battle with the warning, 'if by any means he be missing, then shall thy life be for his life, or else thou shalt pay a talent of silver.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch lay out the 'fictitious tale' in full, noting that the prisoner escaped through the man's negligence, and that the whole is framed to draw a verdict from the king.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke sees the design plainly: 'The drift of this is at once seen; but Ahab, not knowing it, was led to pass sentence on himself.' The reformedJohn Gill pictures the mechanics — the disguised man 'cried unto the king with a loud voice, signifying he had something to say,' at which Ahab stopped to hear the appeal.

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott sets the parable beside its models — Nathan's before David (2 Samuel 12:1-4) and the woman of Tekoa's — and marks the difference in the hearer's response. In David the parable bred repentance; in Ahab it excites 'not compunction, but characteristic sullenness of displeasure,' the same temper he shows over Naboth's vineyard (1 Kings 21:4). The comparison is telling: the same device that broke David's heart only soured Ahab's.

The witnesses read the invented case as an exact mirror of the king's own act. A man was given a prisoner to keep on pain of his own life, and let him slip away 'busy here and there'; Ahab was given Benhadad, a man under God's ban, and let him go for a treaty and a compliment. By framing the charge as a soldier's lapse of duty, the prophet compels Ahab to weigh it coolly, as a matter of plain justice, before the mirror is turned. The detail of the talent of silver, the alternative penalty, only sharpens the point: a solemn trust once accepted may not be bought off cheaply, and the man who lets his charge slip must answer with life or fortune. The trap is baited with the king's own sense of order — and he is about to spring it on himself.
40:
The king pronounces judgment, and the witnesses show how the sentence recoils upon himself. To the plea that the prisoner slipped away 'while thy servant was busy here and there,' Ahab answers, 'So shall thy judgment be; thyself hast decided it.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch give the Hebrew its force — mishpatecha ken, 'thus thy sentence, thou hast decided,' that is, 'thou hast pronounced thine own sentence, and must endure the punishment stated.'

The reformedJohn Gill keeps the human picture: the man pleads that, occupied 'in one thing or another relative to the battle,' his prisoner made his escape — the very excuse of distracted business by which men lose what was entrusted to them. And upon this plea the king delivers his ruling with a magistrate's firmness, unaware that he is drawing up the indictment of his own act at Aphek.

The witnesses read the moment as the perfect closing of the snare. Ahab, judging what he takes to be another man's negligence, allows no appeal: the trust was accepted on stated terms, the terms were forfeited, and the penalty must fall. His justice is sound — that is the point — for it is precisely by the standard of his own sound justice that he will presently be condemned. The parable has worked as Nathan's worked on David: the king has become the judge of his own case, and has judged it against himself.

Behind the human exchange the witnesses hear the divine logic the whole chapter has been pressing. A charge solemnly given, on pain of life, may not be lightly surrendered because one was 'busy here and there'; and Ahab, who let the man of God's ban go free while occupied with flattery and treaty-making, has convicted himself out of his own mouth. The next verse will strip the disguise and turn the verdict upon him; but the sentence is already spoken, and it is Ahab's own. He needs no accuser but himself. It is the peculiar power of the parable, the witnesses observe, that it enlists the offender's own conscience before he suspects his danger: Ahab passes a righteous judgment freely, and only afterward discovers that in condemning the careless keeper he has condemned the careless king. No sentence binds a man so fast as the one his own lips have pronounced.
41:
The disguise comes off, and the witnesses mark the instant of recognition that turns the parable into an accusation. 'He hasted, and took the ashes away from his face; and the king of Israel discerned him that he was of the prophets.' The reformedJohn Gill renders it simply — the prophet 'took off the linen cloth from his eyes, unveiled or unmasked himself' — and Ahab knew him at once for one of the prophetic order.

The wesleyanAdam Clarke confirms the reading he had urged at the disguising: what is 'taken away' is 'the bandage from off his eyes,' a covering 'no doubt of thin cloth, through which he could see, while it served for a sufficient disguise.' The detail matters, for it explains how the prophet could stand by the road and address the passing king without stumbling, yet remain unrecognized until he chose to reveal himself.

The witnesses treat the unmasking as the hinge of the whole encounter. Up to this moment Ahab has been judging a stranger's case; now he sees that the wounded suppliant is a prophet of the Lord, and that the case he has just decided is no soldier's mishap but a message aimed at himself. The recognition is the sword drawn; the parable's protective distance is gone, and the king stands exposed before the very order of men he had once persecuted and had lately learned to fear.

Brief as it is, the verse carries the dramatic weight of a reversal long prepared. Everything — the refused blow, the lion, the effectual wound, the bandage, the invented prisoner, the self-condemning verdict — has led to this lifting of the cloth. The disguise that made Ahab's honesty possible now makes his guilt inescapable, for he cannot pretend he was tricked into an unjust sentence: the judgment was just, and it was his own. The witnesses leave him in that exposed silence, waiting for the word the prophet is about to speak, which will name him plainly as the man his own mouth has condemned. Clarke's small note on the thin, see-through cloth carries a larger weight here — the prophet had needed only covering enough to hide his identity, not to blind himself, so that the whole exchange was conducted in the open, the king freely judging, until the appointed moment for the veil to be lifted.
42:
The prophet delivers the sentence, and the witnesses gather to weigh both its ground and its terrible symmetry. 'Because thou hast let go out of thy hand a man whom I appointed to utter destruction, therefore thy life shall go for his life, and thy people for his people.' The grammatical-historicalKeil and Delitzsch fix the key phrase, ish-chermi, 'the man of my ban' — one on whom God had pronounced the ban, and who by the law was therefore to be put to death (Leviticus 27:29). Ahab has released a man already devoted to destruction, and the doom marked for Benhadad now recoils upon Ahab and Israel.

The grammatical-historicalCharles Ellicott renders it 'a man under my curse,' and compares Ahab's rash lenity to Saul's disobedience in sparing Agag (1 Samuel 15). Whatever its motive — 'partly compassion, partly weakness' — it 'had no right to stand unauthorised between God's judgment and him on whom it was pronounced'; even 'soft-heartedness, as in the case of Eli, may be treason to the cause of righteousness.' The prophet, Ellicott adds, speaks both as a patriot 'jealous of the lenity which left the deadly enemy of Israel unsubdued,' and as 'the representative of God's stern and righteous judgment.'

The wesleyanAdam Clarke and the reformedJohn Gill both note the fulfilment: 'thy life shall go for his life' was accomplished at Ramoth-gilead, where Ahab fell by a Syrian arrow (1 Kings 22:34-35). The puritanMatthew Henry turns the parable's climax on the king — 'Thou art the man' — and extends the doom: as Ahab's life went for Benhadad's, so 'thy people for his people, whom likewise thou hast spared,' fulfilled when Syria afterward ravaged Israel (2 Kings 10:32-33). Henry gathers it into a warning: 'There is a time when keeping back the sword from blood is doing the work of the Lord deceitfully; foolish pity spoils the city.'

The witnesses do not divide on the justice of it, though Ellicott most carefully names the tension — that the very tenderness which looks like virtue was, here, rebellion. Ahab's mercy usurped God's judgment seat; and the sentence that falls on him is the mercy's exact price, life for life and people for people. What he refused to execute upon his enemy is executed upon himself.
43:
The chapter ends with a sullen king, and the witnesses read his heaviness as the mark of a heart reproved but unrepentant. 'The king of Israel went to his house heavy and displeased, and came to Samaria.' The reformedJohn Gill divides the mood: Ahab was displeased 'with the prophet for what he had said, and with himself for what he had done in letting Benhadad go.' The wesleyanAdam Clarke presses the fatal want: he was 'heavy or afflicted because of these dreadful tidings, and displeased with the prophet for announcing them,' whereas 'had he been displeased with himself, and humbled his soul before God, even those judgments might have been averted.'

The puritanMatthew Henry anatomizes the sullenness. Ahab went home 'not truly penitent, or seeking to undo what he had done amiss, but enraged at the prophet, exasperated against God, and yet vexed at himself, every way out of humour, notwithstanding his victory.' Henry sets the two abasements side by side — God 'by his providence had mortified the pride' of Benhadad, and 'by his word cast a damp upon the triumphs' of Ahab — and closes with the psalm's counsel: 'Be wise therefore, O ye kings; serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling' (Ps. 2:10-11).

The wesleyanAdam Clarke takes the verse as the occasion for two long historical notes. The first revives his theory that the 'wall' which fell on the twenty-seven thousand (v.30) may have been a 'burning wind,' chamah rather than chomah, marshalling parallels from Job, Ezekiel, and the destruction of Sennacherib's host to argue for a blasting, suffocating blast such as travellers still report in the East. The second retells, from Froissart, the tale of the six burghers of Calais, whom he had already likened to Benhadad's halter-bearing envoys, as a portrait of 'genuine patriotism.'

The witnesses close the chapter with one accord on its warning. Ahab has seen the God of the hills prove himself the God of the valleys, has been given a victory he did not deserve, and has thrown it away by a mercy God never sanctioned; and now, confronted with his sin, he answers not with repentance but with a black mood. Heaviness is not humility. The reader is left to weigh the difference — that the same word which might have driven Ahab to his knees only sent him home sullen to Samaria, carrying his sentence with him unrepented, toward the arrow that waited at Ramoth-gilead.
